evals.prompt: Design road markings for a Class B rural highway in Kenya with design speed 100 km/h. The road is two-lane two-way with 3.5 m lanes and 1.5 m paved shoulders. Specify: (1) centre line marking type and dimensions, (2) edge line type and dimensions, (3) road stud requirements, (4) marking material recommendation.
evals.expected_output: Centre line: LM02 broken yellow, 3 m mark / 9 m gap, 150 mm wide (Table 4.2, >=80 km/h). Edge line: LM27 continuous white, 150 mm wide. Road studs: centre line bi-directional yellow-yellow at 12 m spacing, edge line bi-directional red-white at 18 m spacing (normal section) reducing to 9 m at no-overtaking sections (Table 13.1/13.2). Material: hot-applied thermoplastic per KS EAS 928-1 with glass beads, initial dry retroreflectivity >=250 mcd/m2/lux (Table 14.1).

assertions.text: Centre line specified as yellow (not white) per Kenya Traffic Act and RDM 6.1 Section 2.10
Centre line dimensions match Table 4.2 for >=80 km/h: 3 m mark, 9 m gap, 150 mm width (LM02)
Edge line specified as continuous white, width 150 mm for higher-category road (LM27)
Road stud spacing references Table 13.1 or 13.2 with appropriate values (12-18 m normal, 9 m no-overtaking)
Material recommendation is thermoplastic (not paint) for this high-speed road
References Kenya RDM 6.1 as the governing standard

evals.prompt: Design junction markings for a signalised 4-arm intersection on an urban arterial with 60 km/h approach speed. The intersection has two through lanes and one right-turn lane on each approach. Specify stop lines, turn arrows, lane markings, and pedestrian crossing markings.
evals.expected_output: Stop line: TM01, 400 mm wide continuous white line, positioned 1.5 m in advance of nearside primary signal. Lane lines: broken white (mark/gap per urban table, 100 mm wide). Lane indication arrows on each approach: straight, left-turn, and right-turn arrows (3.5 m length for approach speed up to 50 km/h or 5.0 m for 51-100 km/h). Pedestrian crossing: white rectangular blocks (500 mm wide, 500 mm gaps, 2.5-4.0 m length), positioned at least 1 m behind stop line. Reference Skill #19 for junction geometry and lane allocation.

assertions.text: Stop line specified as continuous white, 400 mm wide, positioned relative to signal head
Lane indication arrows specified with appropriate length for approach speed
Pedestrian crossing markings specified with zebra bar dimensions
References Skill #19 (Intersection Design) for junction geometric layout
Lane markings transition specified (broken to continuous approaching junction)

evals.prompt: A road marking design is requested for a gravel road (Class D) in Kenya with no existing markings. Design speed is 40 km/h. Determine if markings are appropriate and what provisions are needed.
evals.expected_output: Road markings on unsealed/gravel roads are generally not applicable -- markings require a sealed (paved) surface to be effective and durable. Painted or thermoplastic markings will not adhere to gravel surfaces and will be destroyed rapidly. Escalation trigger: marking on unsealed road. However, road studs may be used on approaches to junctions even if the main road is unsealed -- studs can be fixed to short concrete pads at junction approaches. For the gravel road itself, delineation should rely on road signs (Skill #68) rather than pavement markings.

assertions.text: Identifies that road markings on unsealed/gravel roads are generally not applicable
Triggers escalation or clearly states that markings require a sealed surface
Mentions that road studs may be used on approaches to junctions even if main road is unsealed
Recommends alternative delineation (road signs via Skill #68) for the unsealed road
